topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the embossed lettering on the cover of the old leather-bound volume. His hands, usually steady when presenting arguments in boardrooms, were trembling slightly now. The text inside detailed his entire life—the pivotal moments, the triumphs, the quiet tragedies—but the voice recounting it was undeniably foreign. He reread the passage about his early career, the descriptions of his childhood home, and a cold knot tightened in his stomach. He flipped to the final chapter, a section detailing his supposed philosophical evolution, and his breath hitched. The prose felt like a meticulously crafted imitation, a hollow echo of his actual experiences. He pushed the book away, the sound of the leather scraping the mahogany desk echoing loudly in the silence of his study. He stood up, pacing the length of the room, his gaze darting around the familiar surroundings as if searching for an exit, a truth he couldn't quite locate.

[story 2]

“This is impossible.” Sarah whispered, the words catching in her throat. She stared at the printed timeline spread across the antique table. Every entry, from her first clumsy steps to the moment she decided to pursue astrophysics, felt intensely personal, yet utterly alien. She picked up a pen, her fingers gripping the plastic casing until her knuckles were white. She tried to rewrite a sentence, to inject a genuine memory, but the ink seemed to refuse her command, sliding across the page with a manufactured smoothness. A flush crept up her neck, hot and prickly. She leaned back in her chair, the wood groaning under the pressure, and closed her eyes, trying to force the disparate pieces of information into a coherent narrative, but all she felt was a dizzying sense of dislocation, as if she were watching a performance of her own existence from a great distance.

[story 3]

Marcus sat at the window, the afternoon sun illuminating the dust motes dancing in the air. He held the document, its pages crisp and unnervingly perfect. He read the section describing his greatest vulnerability, the one he had guarded fiercely for years. As his eyes scanned the words—words that resonated with an intimacy he hadn't earned—a strange hollowness expanded in his chest. He dropped the paper onto the rug with a soft thud. He stood, walking to the balcony, breathing in the cool evening air, trying to anchor himself to something solid. The city lights below seemed distant and indifferent. He looked back at the document, and a profound sense of being fundamentally misread washed over him, leaving him rooted to the spot, unable to move toward understanding or away from the unsettling reality presented on the page.
